entries.text: All right, everyone, welcome back.
To data 100 today we're going to continue our.

Journey of how we work with and explore data, and we're going to focus today on text, right? A lot of times we get data that is unstructured text. It's just like, you know, notes, it's records, it's log files, it's any kind of text data that we have to figure out how to like do something meaningful with and process in some interesting and useful way. So we're going to get some tools for how to do that and the main one we're going to work with today is something called regular expressions.

So the announcements before we start. Homework 2A is due on Friday at 11:59 PM. Hopefully you've had a chance to start. If you haven't, highly recommend starting soon. We got plenty of office hours if you get stuck. If you need help, small group tutoring signups are open. Tutoring sections begin this week. If you want one-on-one or one on two help. There are a few sections left that just have a small number of sign ups. Again, take a look at the QR code. There's two options.
Regular where you know attendance is required weekly and you're dropped if you have multiple unexcused absences. There's also like drop in where basically we have weekly signups that open every week.

You sign up for a one off slot for that week and then they reset every week. We also have catch up sessions every Friday from 3:00 to 4:00 at Evans B6. If you feel like you're not quite caught up or you have some confusions on the material or you just want a chance to get a little extra practice, highly recommend checking out the catch up sessions. They're a great opportunity to, you know, fill in on the material that we've been covering this week.
One other thing is for discussion sections, please please please watch the discussion mini lecture.

Before you come to section. So that way we get to make the most of the time we have in section with your TA where you can actually work through problems instead of them talking at you. So I highly recommend or you really should watch the recorded mini lecture before section starts.

And again this week only changed to Josh's office hours. They are Friday from 12:00 to 2:00 PM. And also this week they are on Zoom. And again, that's this week only. Starting next week they'll be back to Wednesday. And as always, but next week, my officers will be on Monday mornings. And you can always check when are office hours where office hours. You can always check the course calendar on the course website.
All right, so where are we right again we saw this dated life cycle diagram.
Where?
Why is this not working OK?

So we have this data life, this sort of like life cycle diagram, this like data science workflow diagram, the version that we have for this class. And again, we're spending most of our time in this sort of bottom right corner of understanding how we explore and work with our data.

So up until now, we've been talking about kind of data processing. Last time we spent a bunch of time on wrangling data. How do you, you know, get the data? How do you process it to get the information you want? Today we're going to talk about text data and learn a tool called regular expressions. And then next time we're going to talk about visualization, right? We've seen some visualization so far and we've seen that they've been super helpful to us in understanding what's going on.
But we haven't talked about how do you actually generate and make those visualizations right? How do you write the code that makes?

The line plot that has, you know, each state as a different color or each region as a different color, how do you make those kind of plots? So we'll talk about that and also how do you decide what plot you use for what scenario?

So quick recap from last time. We talked about these sort of interesting properties of a data set that you want to consider when you're doing your exploratory data analysis. There's structure, right? Like what formatted the data? Is it rectangular? What data types are the different columns, right? We talked about numerical data, quantitative, ordinal and nominal data, and we talked about different file formats. Most of the semester we're going to be working with CSV data, but you will have to work with other file formats. So it's important to keep in mind.
What they look like and that pandas has tools for dealing with it as long as it's rectangular data.

We talked about granularity, which is a super important concept of what does each row represent, right? Usually this is like one of the first things you want to do with your data set is understand what does each row represent? Is each row a person? Is each row a person for a year? Is each row a state for a week? What is the granularity of your data?

We talked about temporality, how the data is situated in time. We learned about Pandas date time data types, and we started talking about faithfulness, right? Which is what is the connection between the data I have and the actual reality that that data is trying to reflect, right? And we saw that there are some questions when it comes to faithfulness.

That we can't answer with just technical tools alone, right? Like are there biases? Are there issues in the underlying data set? What's going on there? But there are some questions that we do have technical tools at our disposal, right? And so these questions of, you know, like, what are the, what is the context of the data? What are the technical decisions we make are deeply intertwined. And it's not just, oh, you do all the ethical, you know, context stuff and you do the technical stuff. You have to think about both together.
So as an example of some just sort of nuts and bolts issues with data, let's take a look at this data set right here. Right I have a bunch of.
Rows and you know, maybe we get this data set. We're not sure exactly what it is. What are some issues with this data that we see?
Let me just take a second.
Look at it.
Hopefully you see at least one. There's actually a lot, but just to highlight a few, right, We have sort of category, state, location, device purchased and we have an ID.
So just to highlight a few errors, First off we have this duplicated row.
Let's see, how do I know this row is duplicated? Because the ID column is the same for both, right? Ideally, if we have an ID, it should probably be unique.
We have state and location that both seem to have the same information, so we don't need both of them.
Probably look through and see if they're equal for all the rows, and if not, what's going on there.
We have some states that are XY. There's no state that has code XY. This is an error in the data.
We have this device column that's always one.
Well, it's always one for the 1st 8 rows. But is it always one for all the rows? Is this column meaningful? Is it interesting? What does it actually mean?

We have this purchased has a missing entry. Some of these have a one, some of them have a 0. Which kind of makes sense, right? It was purchased, It was or it was not. What does it mean for that to be missing? Do we not know whether it was purchased? Was it purchased and returned? Was there some other issue with it? Why is this data missing? We have a -1 in the purchase column.

Again, what does that mean, right? If something was purchased, yes or no, one or zero kind of makes sense. Does -1 mean it was like purchased and returned? Does it mean it was just a return? Does it mean something else entirely?

These are often questions that you have to go back and look from where you got the data from, right? Last time when we were talking about the flu data, I sort of answered a lot of those questions for us because I had spent a bunch of time reading the CDC website and understanding what all the different columns meant.

But when you go get data, when you get it from someone you know you're working with, you have to have a conversation with them. When you get it from somewhere online, you have to make sure you keep in mind where you got it from and be able to read up on the source.

And last but not least, there's this sort of category. Shoe, socks, Pants is misspelled here. If we try to do a group by, we might get one column for PNTS that just has a single row, and then a bunch of columns for pants, right? So we need to somehow normalize and fix this before we continue.
So faithfulness is really about answering the question of do I trust this data?

Right. If we have data that where rows are completely duplicated, then usually that's a good case for dropping the data, right? If I have entire rows that are completely duplicated, then yeah, I can probably just drop one. If I have rows that are duplicated where 1 is different from the other, maybe 2 rows with the same ID and one's different, then again, I have to go back and investigate why that's happening.

Sometimes we have labeling and spare spelling errors. Usually this is something where you want to apply corrections and fix the data. Only ignore if you absolutely have to, right? It's not ideal in that data set with pants, right? If we just drop everything that's a one off, we might lose the data about the pants, right? Not ID.

When you have missing data, which is something that is going to come up all the time because almost every data set you work with will have missing data, you have to think about why the data is missing.
Why is this element missing? And that will affect what decision you make on how to deal with that missing data.
So.

Missing data can show up in a lot of different ways and can show up as a Nan which is not a number. It can show up as an NA in pandas. It can be a zero in a column that should be numeric. There are some cases where you may have a zero that actually means both 0 sometimes, and sometimes that 0 means missing. Josh was talking about a project that he worked on that actually had this happen. There's a link here to the to the paper he wrote where sometimes zero meant missing.
And sometimes 0 actually just meant 0.
So at that point, you can't really trust when it says zero that it actually means zero. You have to take into consideration what that means.
Missing data. This is something that always comes up, so how should you approach it?

Well, there's a few different things you can do. The first is to keep it as just NA or Nan or missing. This is usually a good default thing to do to keep that in your data. You know, if it's like a qualitative column, then maybe just create an entire category that's just missing, right? If it's like shirt, shoes, pants, and then there's a bunch of them that are NA, just like create a separate category that's like missing and analyze that.
In in combination with the others.

Another approach, which is usually not a good idea, sometimes it is but usually not, is to drop records with missing values. That is, anytime you have a missing entry in any of the columns, just eliminate that row.
Why is this bad? Because it introduces bias into your data set.

If you have missing data, say let's say you have a temperature data set and your temperature probe just sort of like went online for a minute at random, then it's probably OK to drop that because the missing data happens at random, right? Every so often it'll miss a point, but you know, if you have continuous data and it misses a couple seconds, probably not the end of the world.
If you have data set on police officers with traffic stops and you have a particular officer who, let's say, happens to never record their own outcomes of the traffic stops that they make.

That could introduce a bias, right? Because if that officer is choosing not to take records of what happens with their traffic stops, then there might be correlations with other things that that officer is doing. So anytime you have missing data, you want to think carefully about what it means, what it and how it fits in with your data and not just drop it. There's also techniques called imputation or interpolation, where basically it's let's fill in that missing data with other values.
The more advanced and sophisticated techniques are kind of beyond the scope of the class. The simplest thing you can do here though.

Is to take a column where, you know, let's say you have a column and some entries in that column are missing. The simplest thing you can do is just say, hey, take those missing values and replace them with the mean or the median of that entire column.

Again, this has issues. In some cases it maybe it makes sense because you say like, yeah, I don't know it, so maybe it's safe to assume it's the average. Whether or not this is a good idea, again depends on the context. So how you deal with missing data, there's no one-size-fits-all answer. The answer is always, it depends. And you have to think about what that data means in the context of your data set, in the context of the problem you're trying to solve, the question you're trying to answer.
OK, so.

With that, let's move on to the main topic for today, which is text processing. So one common thing that's going to come up is needing to clean text data. Text data that you get is almost always messy. It's almost always not quite formatted the way you want, or there's a bunch more information than you need and you want to extract only certain things. So we're going to talk about the STR accessor that gives us that little.
Module with string operations and then we're going to send most of two dimed most of.
Today talking about regular expressions or regex to identify patterns and strengths right? So when you hear regex or regex or regular expression you want to think patterns.

So why do we want to work with text? Well, because it has useful and interesting stuff. So let's look at some examples. What do we want to do with it? Well, the one thing that we often want to do is something called canonicalization. It's really just a fancy way of saying we want to convert data into a standard or form or a canonical form, right? Canonical is just a fancy way of saying I want like a standard, sort of.
You know way of representing this data.
Suppose for example, I have these two tables that have counties, right? And so this data set has information on every county what state it's from.
This data set has the county and the population.
And let's say I want to take this data and aggregate it and compute the total population in each state.
Well, if I want to do that then I need to link these population numbers to the states.
So I need to join these two tables together.

The problem is, if I just joined them on the county column as it is, I'm going to get nothing, right? Why? Because this one says Dwight County. This one just says DeWitt, right? This doesn't have county at the end.
The capitalization is different between the two of them. This one uses and this one uses an ampersand. There's all kinds of issues between these two, right? There's missing periods.
So what we really want is some way of representing this where we have a standard form.
So how might you?
Deal with this data right? How might you deal with the fact that these two representations are different?

Well, what are the problems? The problems are inconsistent spacing. The the problems are county, parish at the end. The problems are these symbols, right? Ampersands and periods that we need to replace. There's a lot of different problems, but we could solve all of those and convert them into a standard representation, right? Maybe we just say, let's use all lowercase letters, remove the spaces, remove the symbols and represent them this way.
So if we could do that, then we can join the columns properly and we'll see an example of that in a moment.
So another thing that we want to do is extract information.
So for example, let's suppose we have log files.

Where that looks something like this, right? We have some website or some web server and we have a bunch of information about people accessing it and we want to know dates and times, right? Let's suppose I'm interested in understanding how often is the Data 100 website accessed? Is it commonly accessed right before an exam? And maybe this effects when course staff should be making updates ahead of common times that people access it?
Well, if I have a bunch of logs that look like this.

Right where this is the line. What I really want is I want to take this line which is just a bunch of text and pull out the date. Well that date lives inside these brackets. So I want to have some way of extracting the stuff that lives in brackets from this string. Right where I want to say I want to take this and get this right. Whereas day, month, year equals 26 Jan 2014 and the time hour, minute, seconds is 104758.
And if I'm being really picky, I can also say the time zone is GMT -8, which I think is like mountain time.
So.

How do we do this? Well, some of this we can do with pandas STR methods, right? We talked about this a little bit a couple weeks ago, but if you remember, in Python, when you have a string, there's a bunch of methods you can use to do stuff with that string. For example, let's say you can convert it from uppercase to lowercase using, you know, S dot lower, S dot upper, right? If S is my string, I can do replacement S dot replace.
Right. And I say I want to replace this with that.
We can do split right? Where I can say OK, take this character, give me everything before, give me everything after.
We can do a substring, we can slice to get, you know, this particular part of it. We can do membership, we can do length. But The thing is, all of these work for one string at a time.

And with pandas data frames, we don't really want to like loop over every single string and do the string operations one by one, right? In general, anytime we have to like loop over something, it's going to be slow. We always want to use the vectorized version of our operations which operate on the entire series at once, right? And generally, you almost always, whenever you can, want to do the whole operation on the whole series at once instead of going 1 by 1.
So pandas has these methods that are vectorized for us.
And we get them by looking at the dot STR method, right? So I say.
My series and I can say dot STR and then I can say dot whatever operation I want to do.

And this applies that function to every element in the series. And we've seen this before, right? If this is our county string, then we can call count this dot STR dot lower and that converts it to lowercase.

So that STR is the accessor for this string module in pennis right? You say dot STR and it gives us access to all the string functions and just like in the for date times we had the dot DT accessor which gives us the date time access to the date time methods. You can do this for strings.
So here is a list of some examples. I'm not going to go over these, we just saw these, but it's important to keep in mind that you can always look these up.
So let's do a little example of how we would work with this data set.
So let's suppose I have.

This data. So I read CSV, the county and state data, I read the county and population data, and again for data frames if I use print they come out looking kind of ugly so I can use display to have them print nicely. So I display the States and I display the populations and they look like this.
So now I want to join these two, but again if I just do a naive merge.
Results not so good. So what I need to do then is I write this function called canonicalized county.
Which is basically saying take the county column right? Take a series with county data and convert it to a standard form. Canonicalize it.

How do I canonicalize it? Well, first I convert everything to lowercase, right? So remember that when I have all these things on separate lines, I'm chaining these methods together, right? I'm doing 1 after the other. So I start by taking my series, convert it to lowercase, then replace any spaces with blanks, right? So basically remove all spaces, replace any ampersands with the word and.
Replace any periods with empty string, remove any periods, remove the word county and remove the word perish.
And for these, remember I'm using lower case because by now I've already converted the string to lower case. All right, so this is a function.

And if I apply it to each of these columns, then I can see that I get the same results for each of these two different data frames, right? They look the same once I apply these operations. And So what I can do is I can define.
New column for each of these with the canonical county.
Right and now I can join on this column.

So I can call state merge populations and because the column name is the same in both of them, I don't really need to bother saying left on equals, right on equals if the name is the same, I can just say on equals right. So this is a special case of the merge function where if both the column names are the same in the left and the right, then I just say on equals and this gives me what I want.
So notice what happens here is that I have these under score X, under score Y.
How come? Well, because Panda sees hey and the left data frame you have a county, a column named county, and the right data frame you have a column named county.

I don't want to have two columns with the same name, so I'm going to preface the one from the left with under score X, and I'm going to preface the OR suffix, and I'm going to suffix the one with the right with under score Y, right? So this is the version from populations, and this county X is the version from states.
All right. Questions on how we canonicalize the county column? Yes.

The question is what do you do with fringe cases and not handling this? Like what if you have a county that doesn't end in county or parish? You can either sort of tackle those fringe cases one by one or you can take sort of reference them against the standard database or you can even say, let me list out a whole bunch of possible words that it could end with and remove all of them. Yeah, the answer is it really depends on your data set. There isn't really A1 size fits all answer. It's a great question though. That kind of thing comes up.
The time, other questions.
Yes.
Uh-huh.

The question is, why is doing it using the STR operations faster? Like what's the difference that makes it so much more efficient? The answer isn't how pandas implements it under the hood. It uses some C code to do with things much faster than you would in a Python loop, yeah.
Yeah, it's like it's not necessarily all simultaneous, it's just like runs using more efficient code.
There are extensions to pandas that can do things in parallel or simultaneous, but they're a little beyond the scope of this class.
OK, so this is an example of canonicalizing. Let's now look at this log file example. So.

Remember that we have this file, right? With a bunch of logs, one line per access, right? This person got this data and maybe we don't care about all this stuff again. We just want to know when are people accessing the website, right? We want to pull out the stuff in brackets, so.
That's.

So how might we go about doing this? Well, I one thing I can see is that hey, I can just look and go, you know, 1234 and count how far into the string this is and slice it right. So again, how did I open this? I used open and read lines and got the data. So I have.
Log lines 0.

Which is the first line of my file as a string and notice it ends with this back slash N character which is again in Python how we represent a new line right? Every line ends with the new line which tells you know the computer go to the next line.
So I can really just say log line to 0 and I can slice it 20 to 31, right? And I could do trial and error, I could count and say, hey, great, I got the date here.
What happens if I do the same thing on the next line?
Well, if I do that on the next line I get.
Something different.
Why? Because this line has a shorter IP address at the start and line, and position 20 means something different here.

Also, this date is only one character instead of two because it's single digit instead of double digit. This is not going to work right. We can't just like look at the same place in the string every time.
We do know that there is a pattern, right? The pattern is there's always a bracket.

And then there's a bunch of slashes, right? So why don't we use that pattern to extract the information? So the way we're going to do it now is not necessarily like the way you should do it. The way you should do it is using regular expressions, which we'll talk about later in lecture. But again, this is just to give you a sense of how you could using the STR methods.
So let's suppose we.
So we'll pull this first line into its own variable so we can play around with it. And I'm going to call split of brackets. So if I say first.
Dot split.
Bracket, open bracket. Then I get a list which is the stuff that I don't care about at the beginning, and then the stuff I want with some extra junk at the end.
Right, so then what I need to do is I need to take that extra, I need to get the second item of the list and then I need to split it again on the closed bracket.
Right. And so that's what this is, right? It's basically first split on open bracket, pull out the second item, then split on closed bracket, pull out the first item.

And then great, I have the date time and now I could go and pull out the day, month by splitting on slash and you know, extracting things one by one. And then I could pull out the year, hour and minute by splitting on the colon and pulling out, you know, the different times. And then I can pull out the time zone and so on and so on. And if I do this.
Then I get the entire.
Data and the way I want it.

So this is kind of annoying to have to like do all of this, you know, splitting and processing just to pull something out. So maybe there's a better way and it turns out there is and it's called regular expressions. So again, this is just one possible solution. One way of doing this right is to do this sort of dot split and then we split up first split on the brackets, then split on the slashes and split on the colon and so on. But we will now look at a better way of.
Extracting this kind of information.
So.

This feels like kind of a hack, right? We're like doing this, all these splits. So what we really want is to somehow say there is a pattern to all these lines. The pattern is there's some stuff I don't care about at the beginning and the end, and then there's brackets, right? And then inside the brackets there's like, you know, the.
Day, then a slash, then the month slash the year and so I know that that's the pattern and so I can encode that pattern.
Pattern into something called a regular expression.
So code that uses replace split is also not very robust, right? So for example, let's suppose I wanted to extract any instance of the word moon from this string.
Right so I have the string moon, moon moon Mon moon and you know this is a reference to this 2013 meme of moon moon. How many people like remember this meme or know of it?
Yeah, it's a little dated. It was pretty great at the time, but it's like super dated.

But basically, let's suppose we have this string, and we want to pull out all these different moon strings and ignore everything else. There's no way to use split and replace to get that information right? But there is a pattern, right? I can describe the pattern to you as something starting with M, ending with N, and having a bunch of OS in the middle.
So anytime you can describe something with a pattern, you can almost always use a regular expression to do it.

So regular expressions are in the RE library in Python, you know, you import RE, you know, just like you import pandas. And you can also access them in the string module for any series. And we can simplify the code using a regular expression.

And again, right now, I don't want you to worry about the details. I want you to just squint and see the big picture here. What's the big picture? The big picture is that we're defining a pattern. What does that pattern look like? It says start with a bracket, pull stuff that is in brackets.
And then we have some digits.
Splash some letters.
Slash some more digits, right? Just like we have some digits slash letters slash more digits and then we have a colon.
And then we have a three sequences of digits, all separated by colons.

Right. So this pattern is our way of expressing that to Python. And by the end of class today, you'll be able to understand like, what all this mumbo jumbo in there actually means. But hopefully you can kind of squint and see that. Yeah, if I take for granted that all this means what I claimed it meant, then yes, this is the pattern that represents that. And this is exactly how regular expressions work.

So a helpful mindset for today is to think about regular expressions kind of like word puzzles, right? If you like doing like Wordles or you know, sudokus or any other kind of thing, regular expressions are basically just another way kind of word puzzle. I love doing wordles. I do them every morning. We're going to there's a little like link to a regex fun game later in class today that I played a bunch over the weekend. Would recommend if you like.

OK, so what's the point of this regular expression thing anyway? Why are we bothering to learn it? In many cases what we want to do is identify or filter certain rows right? Want to find rows that match a pattern and ignore rows that don't.

In other cases, what we want to do is extract data out of strings. We want to take some data that lives in a string and extract certain bits of information. And we've already seen an example of this extraction with that date thing, right? Where we have these log files with all this stuff and want to extract the dates.
So.

What's an example? Let's suppose we have sales data that looks like this, right? Maybe it's recorded from multiple different systems. Maybe those systems change over time. But they always look something like this, right? Where I have some date and then some stuff and, you know, then some amount. But then I have some other rows that have irrelevant junk, right? Like system note. And maybe I only want to know.

The rows that have transactions right? I want to ignore all the other rows right? I want to ignore this manager override manager. Note and notice also that the dates have slightly different formats, right? From 2023 to 2024 the date format changed from using dashes to using slashes.

So. But it's still a date, right? It's still kind of looks the same. So we do see a pattern that we could use to pull out only the rows I've highlighted in yellow and ignore the ones I've highlighted in pink.
And they usually have a dollar.

For the extraction piece, again, we saw an example with those dates. Another example is, let's say we have a data set of political contributions, right then from a candidate's files. Maybe they're generated in a messy way from multiple systems and they look like this. This is the kind of thing that happens a lot. The data that you will see in the real world often looks messy. There's often inconsistencies, but a lot of times there's patterns that you can pick up on, right? And So what are the patterns around these highlighted amounts? Well, they're always numbers.
Sign at the front. Sometimes there's a K, but there might even be an M at the end. But we can always find these dollar amounts and extract them from these lines if we can determine the right pattern.
And so anytime that you have a data set like this, a problem like this where you can define a clear pattern.

You can use a regular expression to do it. So these operations of filtering and extraction using patterns are super common in real world text processing and so that is why we're giving you the tools of regular expressions.

OK. So for the rest of the day, we're going to basically introduce a bunch of different like regular expression concepts, a few at a time, and do some practice on some sliders of kind of working with those concepts we just learned.
All right, So what is a regular expression? It's basically a sequence of characters that specifies a search pattern.
So as an example we have this one here that looks like.
Zero to 9 three times, right? So this again, don't worry about exactly why this means that, but you can kind of see that this is saying any of the digits from zero to 9.
Have to happen three times, then any of the digits.
Zero to 9 two times and then any of the digits zero to 9 four times.
That defines a Social Security number, right? I have digit 3 digits, dash 2 digits, dash 4 digits. That's an SSN.
Right, so this is a regular expression that represents a Social Security number. So regular expressions are patterns that Python, or any programming language really can match to strings.

So regular expression is like the full name. Do you actually do you say regex or regex? There's a debate, there's a link to a Stack Exchange discussion of it. I personally say regex even though it's like regular expression. It's kind of like GIF, GIF, but you know, you'll hear people say different things.
